planting.note: Plant rhizomes 3–4 inches deep in moist, moderately fertile soil in spring; spotted calla lily requires dry summer dormancy (unlike Z. aethiopica which is summer-active) — stop watering when leaves yellow after flowering and keep dry through summer. It is the parent species of many hybrid calla lilies with spotted, translucent-spotted leaves. Suitable for zones 8–11 outdoors; lift and store dry in colder zones.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, moderately fertile, well-drained soil in part shade; spotted calla lily is the primary parent species of the hybrid calla lily group with the characteristic white-spotted, translucent-spotted foliage — the dark-green, arrow-shaped leaves are decorated with numerous white, translucent dots and spots of varying size, creating an attractively patterned foliage effect even before the flowers appear. The flowers are typical calla-form — a creamy-white to pale-yellow or greenish-white spathe surrounding a yellow spadix — smaller and more elegant than the large Z. aethiopica. Unlike Z. aethiopica, it follows a summer-dormant, winter-active pattern (reflecting its summer-dry South African native habitat) and must have dry conditions during summer dormancy to prevent rhizome rot. In zones 8–11 it can remain in the ground with withheld summer water. It is the parent of most hybrid calla lily cultivars with spotted foliage, now available in a wide range of spathe colors from white through yellow, orange, pink, and dark burgundy-purple. Outstanding in containers where the summer dormancy can be easily managed by simply withholding water.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 3–4 inches deep in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in spring; Pentland's calla follows a dry summer dormancy (like Z. albomaculata and Z. rehmannii, not the summer-active Z. aethiopica) — withhold water as foliage yellows after spring flowering and keep dry through the summer. Distinguished from Z. elliottiana by its unspotted foliage and typically larger, deeper-yellow spathe.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, moderately fertile, well-drained soil in part shade; Pentland's calla is a rare but beautiful South African calla lily species from the Drakensberg mountains that produces large, deep-golden-yellow spathes — among the most richly saturated yellows of any Zantedeschia — above plain, unspotted, dark-green, arrow-shaped foliage. The species is less commonly grown than the spotted-leaved Z. elliottiana or Z. albomaculata but has a simpler, cleaner elegance: the combination of the unspotted, deep-green leaves with the vivid pure-gold spathe is uncomplicated and very beautiful. Like the other summer-dormant species (as opposed to Z. aethiopica which is summer-active and evergreen in mild climates), it goes completely dormant in summer, requiring dryness, and re-emerges in autumn or spring depending on climate. The natural habitat is rocky stream banks and moist grasslands of KwaZulu-Natal at mid-altitude, where it experiences cool, moist winters and warm, dry summers. A collector's species that is rarely seen in trade outside specialist bulb suppliers.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall with the top half of the bulb above the soil surface in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Cape forest lily grows, flowers, and sets seed in winter and spring, then goes completely summer dormant — do not water during dormancy. Distinguished from V. bracteata by its wavy-edged, glaucous leaves and preference for more alkaline, sandy, well-drained conditions (V. bracteata prefers humus-rich, moist soil).

directions: Plant bulbs (upper half exposed) in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade; Cape forest lily is the drier-habitat Veltheimia species — growing naturally in the sandy, well-drained, somewhat alkaline substrates of the coastal scrubland and sandy flats of the Western Cape rather than the more moisture-retaining, humus-rich soils where V. bracteata thrives. Like V. bracteata it is a winter-growing bulb, emerging with the Cape rains in autumn and flowering in late winter (July–September in South Africa, January–March in the Northern Hemisphere), producing the same characteristic dense, bottle-brush-like raceme of pendant, tubular flowers on a sturdy, mottled-purple scape — but in slightly different, usually more pink-flushed or paler-pink shades. The leaves are distinctly glaucous (blue-gray-green) and wavy-edged, contrasting with the glossier, deeper-green flat leaves of V. bracteata. Summer dormancy is complete and mandatory — both species die back entirely and must be kept bone dry. A plant for the specialist collector of South African winter-growing bulbs, particularly valuable for mild-winter Mediterranean-climate gardens in zones 9–11.

planting.note: Plant bulbs just below the soil surface in fall in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; coral drops grows, flowers, and goes dormant on the Cape winter-rainfall schedule — emerging in autumn, flowering in late winter and spring, then going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer. The name 'bulbifera' refers to the bulbils produced in the leaf axils, a common propagation strategy of Cape bulbs.

directions: Plant bulbs near the soil surface in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; coral drops is one of the most colorful of the Lachenalia species — producing the characteristic two strap-shaped, often purple-spotted, somewhat succulent leaves and in late winter through spring (February–April in cultivation), dense, upright, bottlebrush-like racemes of pendant, tubular bells in a striking combination of coral-red to orange-red at the top of each tube fading through yellow to greenish-yellow at the tube base — a three-toned color scheme of warm coral, yellow, and green that is visually complex and beautiful. The bright, tricolor flower tubes are surrounded by slightly smaller outer perianth segments. It produces small bulbils in the leaf axils that fall and generate new plants when conditions are suitable — an efficient natural propagation strategy adapted to the Cape fynbos. Outstanding for container culture in zones 8–11, where the pot can be kept dry in summer. One of the most colorful and largest-flowered of the commonly grown lachenalias.

subcategory: uniquely colored South African Cape bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs just below the soil surface in fall in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; like all lachenalias it follows the Cape winter-rainfall growth cycle — emerging in autumn, blooming in late winter and spring, then going completely summer dormant. Keep dry in summer. This species is notable for its unique turquoise-green flower color, one of the only true blue-green (teal) flower colors in the plant kingdom.

directions: Plant bulbs near the soil surface in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; turquoise lachenalia is one of the most botanically remarkable flowering bulbs in the world for a single, astonishing reason — its flowers are turquoise-green to blue-green, a color so unusual in the plant kingdom that it qualifies as genuinely unique among commonly cultivated bulbs. True blue-green (teal) flowers are exceedingly rare in nature; this color is achieved through a combination of unusual pigment concentrations in the pendant, tubular bells that gives the entire flower head a distinct turquoise to blue-green hue that appears almost artificial or painted when seen for the first time. The effect is unlike any other flower color in the bulb garden. The flowers appear in dense, upright racemes of 10–20 pendant tubular bells on stems 6–10 inches tall in late winter and early spring above two strap-shaped, somewhat spotted, succulent-tinged leaves. Like all Cape Lachenalia, it requires the strict winter-growth, summer-dormancy schedule of the Mediterranean-climate Cape fynbos. A collector's plant of extraordinary botanical interest for its completely unprecedented flower color.
